Energy Markets

Maryland Electric Prices Are Climbing Again. A Dirty HVAC Quietly Doubles the Damage

May 12, 2026 6 min read

If your last BGE, Pepco, Delmarva, or SMECO bill made you blink twice, you are not imagining things. Maryland electric supply rates have moved up in nearly every billing cycle for the past three years, and the latest PJM capacity auction signaled that more increases are baked in for 2026 and 2027. Most building owners are spending hours shopping suppliers, switching plans, and arguing with billing departments.

Almost nobody is doing the one thing that actually moves the meter: cleaning the equipment that is burning the power.

Why Maryland Power Prices Keep Going Up

~24%
Avg. MD electric rate increase since 2022
$329/MW-day
PJM capacity auction clearing price
~40%
MD electricity generated by natural gas
~$0.12
Avg. MD commercial $/kWh, all-in

There is no single villain here. Five things are stacking on top of each other:

  • PJM capacity auctions cleared at record highs. PJM is the regional grid operator that serves Maryland. The capacity charge is the money paid to generators just to be ready to run. That number cleared roughly 9x higher than the prior auction, and Maryland customers pay a piece of it on every kWh.
  • Data centers are pulling power out of the region faster than new generation is being built. Loudoun County alone has more data center load than most US states. That demand spills over into the same wholesale market Maryland buys from.
  • Coal and older gas plants keep retiring without 1-for-1 replacements. Less supply chasing more demand is the textbook recipe for higher prices.
  • Natural gas, which fuels about 40% of Maryland's grid, is being pulled toward LNG export terminals. When wholesale gas moves up, wholesale power moves with it.
  • Transmission upgrade costs are rising. The wires and substations that move power around the PJM footprint need billions in upgrades, and the charge for that shows up as transmission line items on your bill.

None of those drivers reverse in the next 12 months. So if you are running a commercial building in Maryland, the practical question is not whether to fight your supplier. It is how much energy you can stop wasting before the next rate hike lands.

The Quiet Number Most Owners Miss

A typical 30,000 to 60,000 square foot Maryland commercial building runs roughly 200,000 to 350,000 kWh per year on cooling and ventilation alone. At today's all-in commercial rate of about $0.12 per kWh, that is anywhere from $24,000 to $42,000 per year just in HVAC electricity.

That number is the baseline. The catch is that almost no building actually runs at the efficiency the equipment was rated for on day one. Coils foul. Filters load up. Belts slip. Refrigerant levels drift. Economizers stick. Each one is small. Together they add up to a real second bill that owners pay every month and never see itemized.

How a Dirty HVAC Multiplies Your Rate Hike

This is the part most owners do not realize. When power prices climb, every inefficiency in your HVAC system gets more expensive in lockstep. A 15% efficiency loss on a 12 cent rate stings. A 15% efficiency loss on a 15 cent rate is a budget problem.

Here is where the energy actually leaks on a rooftop unit or split system that has not been touched in 18 months or more:

IssueTypical Efficiency HitWhat It Costs Per $10K of HVAC Spend
Dirty condenser coil20% to 30%$2,000 to $3,000 per year wasted
Clogged air filter10% to 15%$1,000 to $1,500 per year wasted
Dirty evaporator coil10% to 20%$1,000 to $2,000 per year wasted
Slipping or worn belt5% to 10%$500 to $1,000 per year wasted
Low or overcharged refrigerant10% to 20%$1,000 to $2,000 per year wasted
Stuck or broken economizer5% to 15%$500 to $1,500 per year wasted
Thermostat drift / poor setpoints5% to 10%$500 to $1,000 per year wasted

You will not see all of those on the same unit. But on the typical small or mid-size commercial building we walk into, we usually find three or four of them at once. That is how a building running $30,000 a year on HVAC quietly turns into $36,000 to $40,000.

Rule of thumb we use in the field: if your rooftop units have not been professionally cleaned and tuned in the last 18 months, plan on 10% to 20% of your HVAC electricity bill being pure waste. At Maryland's current commercial rates, that is real money every quarter.

What Cleaning and Tuning Actually Means

"HVAC cleaning" can mean anything from a $99 filter swap to a full service overhaul. The version that actually moves your bill is what EmPOWER Maryland calls a Building Tune-Up. On each unit, that includes:

  • Pressure-washing and chemically cleaning the condenser and evaporator coils
  • Replacing filters with the correct MERV rating for the unit
  • Checking and adjusting refrigerant charge
  • Inspecting and replacing belts, bearings, and contactors as needed
  • Cleaning blower wheels and drain pans
  • Testing and recalibrating economizer dampers and outside air settings
  • Verifying thermostat setpoints and schedules match how the space is actually used
  • Capturing pre and post readings so the savings are documented, not guessed at

That is the work that turns a 15% efficiency loss back into 0%. Done across an entire building's HVAC fleet, owners typically see a 5% to 20% reduction in cooling and ventilation electricity, year after year, until the equipment fouls up again.

Why Maryland Owners Pay Nothing for This Right Now

EmPOWER Maryland is a statewide ratepayer-funded program. Every BGE, Pepco, Delmarva, and SMECO customer pays a small charge on their bill that funds energy-efficiency rebates. For qualifying commercial buildings, that fund covers up to 100% of the cost of a professional HVAC Tune-Up. Done correctly and submitted through an approved trade ally, the customer pays $0 out of pocket.

That is the part most owners do not realize. You have already paid for the tune-up. The question is only whether you actually claim it before someone else's building does.

The Math, Plainly

Take a midsize office park running about $30,000 per year on HVAC electricity, sitting at roughly 15% efficiency loss across the fleet. That is about $4,500 per year in waste at today's rates. If Maryland commercial rates climb another 10% over the next 18 months, that same waste turns into about $4,950 per year, then $5,400, then more.

A tune-up campaign through EmPOWER Maryland clips most of that waste in one season and resets the baseline. It does not lock in your supply rate, and it does not stop PJM from doing what PJM does. It does make sure that whatever rate you end up paying, you are paying it on the smallest possible kWh number.

What to Do Before Cooling Season Hits

  1. Schedule a free walkthrough. We come on site, inventory every HVAC unit, and tell you which ones qualify under your utility's EmPOWER program. No cost, no obligation.
  2. Have us run the rebate paperwork. The EmPOWER tune-up forms have caught more building owners than the work itself. We handle BGE, Pepco, Delmarva, and SMECO submissions in-house.
  3. Get the work done before July. Once peak cooling hits, every commercial HVAC tech in the state is booked. The owners who tune up in May and June pay the same $0, and ride out the summer on cleaner equipment.
  4. Keep filters on a quarterly cycle. Even after the tune-up, a clogged filter can wipe out a real chunk of the gains in 6 months. Put it on the calendar.

The Bottom Line

Maryland power prices are going up. That part is out of any one owner's control. What is fully in your control is whether your building keeps paying those higher rates on top of 10% to 20% wasted kWh, or pays them on a clean, properly tuned HVAC system that earns back every dollar of supply you buy.

EmPOWER Maryland is currently funding the entire fix. Most Maryland owners will let it expire unused. The ones who do not will spend the next 24 months looking like they got a head start on every rate hike that comes through.

Lock In Your Building's Efficiency Before the Next Rate Hike

Free walkthrough. We inventory every unit, model the savings, and handle every step of the EmPOWER Maryland paperwork. You pay $0.
